records.content:
During this project, we first explored several datasets as a group and selected those related to education and lifestyle. These datasets allowed us to examine how different lifestyle factors may influence educational performance. Based on this, we aimed to answer the question whether a strict lifestyle or a more balanced lifestyle leads to better academic outcomes. To investigate both perspectives, we created eight visualizations for each side, presenting evidence that either supports or challenges the effectiveness of each lifestyle.

Our findings suggest that a stricter lifestyle is generally associated with better educational performance. However, the data also indicates that this approach may lead to higher levels of stress. This highlights a trade-off between academic success and well-being, suggesting that while strict routines can improve performance, they may come at a personal cost.

We chose four datasets from Kaggle, which all include variables about lifestyle and education.

Dataset 1: Student lifestyle and academic performance (

https://​www​.kaggle​.com​/datasets​/steve1215rogg​/student​-lifestyle​-dataset)

Dataset 2: Gaming dataset (

https://​www​.kaggle​.com​/datasets​/aiexplorer77​/gaming​-vs​-academic​-performance)

Dataset 3: Alcohol consumption dataset (

https://​www​.kaggle​.com​/datasets​/uciml​/student​-alcohol​-consumption).

Dataset 4: Student Habits vs Academic Performance (

https://​www​.kaggle​.com​/datasets​/jayaantanaath​/student​-habits​-vs​-academic​-performance​/data)

Variables used in the data story are listed and categorized below.

Dataset 1 (D1) - Student lifestyle and academic performance:

Stress level (ordinal): Level of stress reported in 3 levels - low, medium and high

GPA (ratio): Average grade on a 4.0 max scale

Dataset 2 (D2) - Gaming vs Academic Performance:

Gaming hours (ratio): Average gaming hours per day

Reaction time (ratio): Average response time

Grades (ratio): Final grade score from 0-100 scale

Grade category (nominal): classifies students’ performance into three ordered levels: fail, sufficient and good

Dataset 3 (D3) - Student Alcohol Consumption:

Study time (ordinal): Weekly study time in four categories (1 = <2 hours, 2 = 2-5 hours, 3 = 5-10 hours, 4 = > 10 hours)

Final grade (ratio): Final grade (G3) on 0-20 scale

Weekday alcohol consumption (ordinal): Alcohol consumption during weekdays rated from very low (1) to very high (5)

Weekend alcohol consumption (ordinal): Alcohol consumption during weekends rated from very low (1) to very high (5)

Absences (ratio): Number of school absences

Paid extra classes (nominal): Whether the student attended paid extra educational support (Yes/No).

Course (nominal): whether the observation is for Math or Portuguese classes (only Math and Portugese data available, in 2 separate datasets)

Dataset 4 (D4) - Student Habits vs Academic Performance:

Study hours per day (ratio): Average daily study time.

Exam score (ratio): Final examination score.

To preprocess the above datasets, we first inspected the descriptive details of each dataset and remove irrelevant variables. Then, we checked for missing values and duplicated records which we removed. To handle missing values, we used median imputation for numerical variables and mode imputation for categorical variables. Besides, we also checked for outliers. Lastly, we removed illogical datapoints, for example those who have grades above 100 (the maximum of grading scale used) in dataset 2.

Specific preprocessing attempts in perspective 1

In dataset 1, we encoded students’ grade variables into categorical variables called Grade Group using percentile tertile grouping (pandas.qcut), which divides students’ grade into three approximately equal-sized groups: Low, Medium, High.

Specific preprocessing attempts in perspective 2

In dataset 3, we generalized the grades from 1-20 to 1-10. Also, we decided to make a new column called ‘Alcohol’ to generalize alcohol consumption over the entire week by adding the columns workday alcohol (Walc) and weekend alcohol (Dalc) together and dividing the new number by two, so it remains within a 1 to 5 scale.

\text{Normalized Study Time}
=
\frac{x-x_{\min}}{x_{\max}-x_{\min}}\times10

The x is the original study time,  x_{\min}  and  x_{\max}  are the minimum and maximum study time values within each dataset. This allows the comparison between datasets in study time to become meaningful and relative.
For example: In Dataset 4, study time ranges from 0 to 8.3 study hours per day. A student who studies 6 hours is re-scaled as:\frac{6.0 - 0}{8.3 - 0} \times 10 = 7.23

Then, a study time of 6 is represented as 7.23 on the 0-10 scale.

What factors help improve academic performance has always been a crucial question of generations of students and also researchers in the educational field (e.g. 

Adelantado-Renau et al. (2019), 

Singh et al. (2018), 

Camacho-Morles et al. (2021)).

In some perspectives, a successful study method is investing most of your daily time to study, without any distractions from social activities, gaming or entertainment. Previous studies show that having serious learning behaviors for every day plays an important role in grades (

Credé & Kuncel (2008)). However, student life is not just studying. They also spend time socializing and participating in other physical or extracurricular activities. Studying in a strict way might not be the most effective method to acquire high scores. Studies found that sports participation and extracurricular activities may also benefit students’ academic achievement (

Barbosa et al. (2020),  

Gilman et al. (2003)).

Therefore, this data story aims to investigate whether living a balanced or strict lifestyle would be more beneficial to students’ study habits, and the role of external variables such as study hours and gaming in the relationship between type of lifestyle and academic performance. Specifically, two central perspectives in this story are “Having a strict lifestyle (e.g. only focusing on studying and not participating in leisure activities) improves students’ academic performance.” and “Having a balanced lifestyle (e.g. combining studying and participating in leisure activities) improves students’ academic performance.”
Strict lifestyle means focusing more on studying and reducing leisure activities. In this part, we discuss how strict lifestyle affects academic performance.

Students spending more time studying tend to achieve higher academic performance.

%run ../plots/study_time_plot.ipynb



Figure 1. Average Study Time by Grade Group Across Four Datasets

Study time was chosen as the first indicator of a strict lifestyle because it shows the students’ investment and prioritization of learning. Across four datasets, higher study time is consistently correlated with higher academic performance. However, the relationship is not similar across subjects, as shown in the Math vs Portuguese visualization. That is because of different learning demands of two subjects, as mathematics often requires more structured problem-solving tactics, while language relies heavily on continuous exposure, self-practicing and application in reality (

Foster (2023), 

Zhang & Zou (2020)). However, study time does not determine academic success, it also depends on learning strategies, quality, attention, and students’ motivation (

Rosário et al. (2012)).

Students with fewer absences tend to achieve higher academic performance.

%run ../plots/attendance_plot.ipynb



Figure 2. Average Absences by Grade Group in Mathematics and Portuguese

Absence was selected as another indicator of a strict lifestyle, as it reflects students’ academic discipline. In both Mathematics and Portuguese, students in high grade group show fewest average absences, while medium and low grade groups are absent more frequently. This is supported by 

Credé et al. (2010) that absenteeism can lead to lower grades, as missing classes reduces learning opportunities and classroom engagement.

Students who get higher grades tend to combine intensive study time with extra classes.

%run ../plots/extra_class_plot.ipynb



Figure 3. Average Final Grade by Study Time and Extra Classes

Extra classes were included as another indicator of strict lifestyle because it reports students’ additional investment beyond mandatory classes. However, this pattern differs by subject. For Mathematics, students attending extra classes showed higher grades as study time increased. This is because extra classes or study time may not fully explain mathematical grades; their performance may also depend on learning strategies and conceptual understanding (

Uyandıran & Tarım (2024), 

TURGUT (2021)). For Portuguese, students studying more without extra classes tend to achieve higher grades than those who combine both of them, while the latter showed a fluctuating pattern. This difference lies in the subject characteristics: mathematics requires teacher’s problem-solving methods and guidance (

Alegre-Ansuategui et al. (2017), 

Uyandıran & Tarım (2024)), while with foreign languages, self-study and practice in reality will be more effective in improving students’ language skills (

Nakata (2016), 

Zhang & Zou (2022)).

Despite possibly affecting mental well-being, strictly following an intense study schedule brings fruitful result

%run ../plots/pers1_subarg4.ipynb





Figure 4 Grade Distribution by Stress Level

Overall, data shows that high stress correlates with high grades. Since strict, intensive study schedules are a very plausible reason students report high stress (rather than e.g. financial stress or personal issues, given this is a student lifestyle dataset), figure 4 visually supports the idea that a strict lifestyle pays off academically. This phenomenon can be explained by the concept of eustress, which is a type of positive stress that result in positive outcome, which stems from the Yerkes-Dodson Law stating that ‘‘increasing stress is beneficial to performance until some optimum level is reached…’’ (

Le Fevre et al. (2003)). However, it is worth noting that grades spread quite widely in the high stress group, which can be seen in the “With datapoints” version of the above figure, thus the pattern may not apply universally every time.
The balanced lifestyle perspective focuses on combining studying with leisure activities. In this section, we examine how alcohol consumption and gaming affect academic performance.

The first argument supports the perspective that having a balanced lifestyle can improve students’ academic performance by reducing harmful behaviors such as excessive alcohol consumption. The article from 

Carrell et al. (2011) shows that higher alcohol consumption is associated with lower student achievement, especially among high-performing students.

%run ../plots/alcohol_consumption_plot1.ipynb



Figure 5: Distribution of alcohol consumption

%run ../plots/alcohol_consumption_plot.ipynb



Figure 6: Final grade by average alcohol consumption

The two visualizations show a weak negative correlation between final grades, average alcohol consumption, and Math/Portuguese. Across both figures, a slight decline in performance can be observed as alcohol consumption increases, with the effect appearing somewhat stronger for Portuguese than for Mathematics. In addition, figure 5 suggests that alcohol consumption levels between 4 and 5 are underrepresented, which may have influenced these results. Overall, our findings partially support 

Carrell et al. (2011), which also found that higher alcohol consumption was associated with lower grades, although the relationship in our data appears less pronounced.

The second argument supporting the balanced lifestyle perspective is that increased time spent gaming may negatively affect grades, but can simultaneously enhance response times, which may in turn improve educational performance. This claim is supported by research from 

Prabu Kumar et al. (2020), which found that faster response times are associated with improved cognitive performance. Additionally, 

Chandra et al. (2016) demonstrates that gaming can significantly improve response times. Taken together, these findings suggest that gaming may indirectly support certain aspects of academic performance through cognitive enhancement, although excessive gaming may have negative effects on grades.

%run ../plots/gaming_stats_3d.ipynb



Figure 7: Reaction time vs Grades vs Gaming Hours

%run ../plots/gaming_response_time.ipynb



Figure 8: How reaction time influences grades

The two figures suggest that reaction time does not strongly influence grades, as the data shows no clear or consistent pattern. In contrast, time spent gaming appears more related to grades, with a more noticeable trend. Combined, these results do not support reaction time as an important predictor of academic performance in this dataset. This is interesting because it contrasts with findings from 

Prabu Kumar et al. (2020), which report a positive relationship. The study suggests that faster reaction times may indicate quicker information processing, better attentional control, and more efficient cognitive functioning, all of which can support learning and test performance.

We used bar charts to compare average study time across low, medium, and high grade groups. Four datasets were included to highlight consistent patterns, showing that higher-performing students tend to invest more time in studying. Comparisons between different schools were not made. To improve comparability, study time was rescaled to a 0–10 scale, clarifying its relationship with scores. In the Student Alcohol Consumption dataset, data was merged into a single table with a “Subject” column to distinguish between subjects.

We used grouped bar charts to compare the average absent number of Mathematics and Portuguese students across grade groups. The x-axis indicates the students’ grade group, while the y-axis shows the average absences. The legend was used to encode two subjects, helping the audience distinguish whether the absence pattern differs between two subjects.

A line chart was used to illustrate how the relationship between combined study time and average final grade differs depending on whether students attended extra classes or not. Study time is shown on the x-axis, average final grade is represented as the y-axis, and legend color distinguishes students who attended extra classes and those who did not attend, making differences in trend easily comparable.

We believe median is the most suitable statistic to represent the typical performance of the groups because the size of different stress groups are not equal. Extreme values are more likely to occur by chance in larger groups and to influence the mean. From that, boxplot is chosen as it not only displays the median effectively but also shows the distribution of students’ grades in each group. Two versions of the visualization (with and without datapoints) are available for readers who want to inspect the spread of outliers in detail. The contrast of colors is utilized: a darker shade of red delivers an increasing level of stress.

For figure 5 a histogram is used as the main mark to compare alcohol consumption levels and percentages. Position, height, and color encode the values, with separate colors for the Mathematics and Portuguese datasets. The data includes ordinal, nominal, and ratio variables, which makes the bar chart a suitable choice for clear category comparison.

Figure 6 uses boxplots to show the distribution of final grades across alcohol consumption levels. Position, size, and color are used to display the values and distinguish the two datasets. The plot also includes outliers, median values, and spread, giving a fuller view of how grades vary across categories.

Figure 7 shows the relationship between grades, reaction time, and gaming hours in a 3D scatter plot, with each axis clearly labeled and a note that lower reaction times indicate faster responses. The points are colored by grade category using a traffic-light palette, and only 500 rows are randomly sampled to keep the figure easier to read.

Figure 8 builds on Figure 7 by using a 2D scatter plot to show the relationship between grades and reaction time, while the violin plots highlight how gaming hours are distributed across grade categories. The consistent color scheme preserves visual continuity across the figures, and the random sample of 800 rows was used to keep the visualization clear and readable.

On June 23rd 2026, our group presented the story draft to the class and received some peer feedback, which mainly focused on strengthening the interpretation of our visualizations and showing the distinct comparisons across datasets more clearly:

Normalize the study time into the same scale in figure 1.

Focus the study discipline explanation on Dataset 3 (absence level), as the other datasets show similar attendance percentages across groups.

Clarify and emphasize the justification for stress level in the strict lifestyle.

Simplify the alcohol consumption intensity by reducing the repeated patterns in the bar chart.

Remove the boxplot from grades versus reaction time with a gaming scatter plot.

Following the feedback, our team reviewed the project carefully and made changes accordingly.

All members found datasets, did preprocessing and wrote reflection by themselves.
Ian handled technical tasks such as Github organizing and website building. Ian wrote the conclusion, he and David also created graphs and wrote arguments and reflection in perspective 2. Mai Anh wrote introduction, Ngoc wrote peer feedback, and they created graphs and arguments for perspective 1.
